title: Laurelin
text:
Preface
Calculus made sense to me in a way that most mathematics did not. Not because it was easy — it was not — but because it described things that changed. Velocity and acceleration. The area under a curve. The box dimensions that maximize volume while minimizing material. I remember thinking: if I can write an equation for a falling object, I should be able to write one for sales figures, service orders, or the demand forecasts that actually drive business decisions.
I could not. I did not know where to start.
The equations we worked with in calculus had tidy, well-behaved variables. The variables I cared about — orders, failures, costs — did not come pre-packaged as functions. I did not know how to turn a spreadsheet into mathematics. I took a course that covered regression and did not recognize it as the tool I had been looking for. I finished my undergraduate degree in financial engineering with the vague sense that the mathematics I had learned was somehow adjacent to the problems I wanted to solve, but not quite touching them.
That gap stayed with me for a long time.
I went to graduate school in economics knowing almost nothing about the field. What drew me in was the mathematics — more of it, and different from what I had seen in financial engineering. I had always found it easy to develop an interest in new areas when the underlying structure was rigorous, and economics turned out to have plenty of that. I started working seriously with data — analysis, visualization, pattern recognition. The original question, the one about describing real business variables with equations, receded into the background. It felt like a different project.
It was not until my PhD that I realized I had been circling the same problem the entire time. Optimization, it turns out, is precisely the discipline of writing equations that describe what we want and finding the inputs that satisfy them. Machine learning is, at its core, the application of that idea to data. The regression I had not recognized as useful in my undergraduate years was the same regression I was now extending, formalizing, and building new variants of for my dissertation.
This book is my attempt to build that connection explicitly — to draw the straight line between calculus and ML that took me twenty years to see.

The mathematics here start from first principles and build toward the contributions of my PhD thesis: a family of Support Vector Regression models that optimize percentage-error loss functions directly. The early chapters require nothing beyond multivariable calculus. The later chapters are graduate-level, but the goal throughout is the same: to show where the equations come from and why they take the form they do.
Code examples appear in paired R and Python tabs. Advanced derivations are in collapsible sections — they are there for completeness, but skipping them does not break the argument.

title: 1  From Spreadsheet to Function
text:
2 From Spreadsheet to Function
What do you do when a spreadsheet is no longer enough?
Not because the data is too large. Not because the formulas are too slow. Because the question you are asking cannot be answered with a formula you can write by hand — and you know it.
This is where most data work actually starts. Not with a model, not with an algorithm. With a question that a pivot table cannot answer, and the uncomfortable realization that something more systematic is needed.
This book is about building that something from scratch.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 1  From Spreadsheet to Function
section: 2.1 A company’s electricity bill, by the half-hour
text:
2.1 A company’s electricity bill, by the half-hour
Consider a company that operates in Victoria, Australia. Its infrastructure runs on electricity — servers, cooling systems, office equipment. Every half-hour, the grid records how much power the company draws. Every half-hour, the wholesale price of that electricity changes based on how much everyone else is drawing at the same time.
If you can predict demand before it happens, you can act: pre-scale servers, shift workloads, negotiate contracts. If you cannot, you react — and reacting is expensive.


Code
library(here)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

1vic_elec <- read_csv(here::here("data", "vic_elec.csv"))

2vic_sample <- vic_elec |>
  filter(Time >= as.POSIXct("2014-01-01"),
         Time <  as.POSIXct("2014-01-15"))

ggplot(vic_sample, aes(x = Time)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Demand, colour = "Demand (MW)"),
            linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Temperature * 30, colour = "Temperature (°C × 30)"),
            linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Demand (MW)" = "#2166ac",
               "Temperature (°C × 30)" = "#d6604d")
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Demand (MW)", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")



1

Loaded from the book’s data/ folder; original source is the tsibbledata R package (half-hourly Victorian electricity demand, 2012–2014).

2

We filter to January 2014 — peak summer in Australia — where demand is highest and most variable.










Figure 2.1: Victorian electricity demand and temperature over a two-week window. Demand tracks temperature, but not perfectly — and the gap between them is where the forecasting problem lives.





The pattern is visible but imprecise. Temperature drives demand — air conditioning is the dominant load — but the relationship shifts with the day of the week, with holidays, with whether it was already hot the day before. A line through a scatter plot will not capture that. A lookup table will not generalize to next week.
What we need is a function — one that takes what we know now (temperature, hour, day type) and returns a prediction we can act on.

RPython




Code
library(readr)

vic_elec <- read_csv(here::here("data", "vic_elec.csv"))

vic_elec |>
  select(Time, Demand, Temperature, Holiday) |>
  slice_head(n = 8) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
               caption = "First eight rows of `vic_elec`.")

import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path

def _project_root(markers=("_quarto.yml", "renv.lock")):
    for d in (Path.cwd(), *Path.cwd().parents):
        if any((d / m).exists() for m in markers):
            return d
    return Path.cwd()

1vic_elec = pd.read_csv(_project_root() / "data" / "vic_elec.csv")

(
  vic_elec[["Time", "Demand", "Temperature", "Holiday"]]
  .head(8)
)



1

Loaded from the book’s data folder; original source is the tsibbledata R package (half-hourly Victorian electricity demand, 2012–2014).






Table 2.2



                  Time       Demand  Temperature  Holiday
0           2012-01-01  4382.825174        21.40     True
1  2012-01-01 00:30:00  4263.365526        21.05     True
2  2012-01-01 01:00:00  4048.966046        20.70     True
3  2012-01-01 01:30:00  3877.563330        20.55     True
4  2012-01-01 02:00:00  4036.229746        20.40     True
5  2012-01-01 02:30:00  3865.597244        20.25     True
6  2012-01-01 03:00:00  3694.097664        20.10     True
7  2012-01-01 03:30:00  3561.623686        19.60     True







The dataset has 52608 rows — one per half-hour from 2012 to 2014. Every row is a moment when someone, somewhere, made a decision about how much electricity to use. Together, they describe a system with memory, seasonality, and noise.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 1  From Spreadsheet to Function
text:
2.2 House prices across California
The second running example is quieter. No time index, no recurring pattern. Just 20,640 census blocks from California in the early 1990s, each described by eight numbers: median income, house age, average rooms, average bedrooms, population, average occupancy, latitude, longitude. The target is median house value.


Code
1library(readr)
library(ggplot2)

2California <- read_csv(here::here("data", "california_housing.csv"))

ggplot(California, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude,
                       colour = log(median_house_value))) +
  geom_point(size = 0.4, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "plasma",
                         name = "log(value)") +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1.3) +
  labs(x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)



1

The California Housing data ships as a CSV in the book’s data/ folder; it is the same dataset scikit-learn exposes via fetch_california_housing().

2

The full dataset has 20,640 rows. We use it as-is here; experiments later use a stratified subsample for speed.










Figure 2.2: Median house value across California census blocks, plotted by geographic location. Values are compressed at the upper end — a pattern that makes percentage error a more natural metric than raw dollar error.






RPython




Code
library(readr)

California <- read_csv(here::here("data", "california_housing.csv"))

California |>
  slice_head(n = 8) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
               caption = "First eight rows of the California Housing dataset.")

Code
import pandas as pd
1from sklearn.datasets import fetch_california_housing

cal = fetch_california_housing(as_frame=True).frame
2cal = cal.rename(columns={
    "MedInc": "median_income", "HouseAge": "house_age",
    "AveRooms": "average_rooms", "AveBedrms": "average_bedrooms",
    "Population": "population", "AveOccup": "average_occupancy",
    "Latitude": "latitude", "Longitude": "longitude",
    "MedHouseVal": "median_house_value",
})
3cal["median_house_value"] = cal["median_house_value"] * 100_000
cal.head(8)



1

fetch_california_housing() returns the same data scikit-learn ships.

2

We rename to the book’s snake_case columns so the table matches the R tab.

3

scikit-learn reports MedHouseVal in units of $100,000; we rescale to raw dollars to match data/california_housing.csv.






Table 2.4



   median_income  house_age  ...  longitude  median_house_value
0         8.3252       41.0  ...    -122.23            452600.0
1         8.3014       21.0  ...    -122.22            358500.0
2         7.2574       52.0  ...    -122.24            352100.0
3         5.6431       52.0  ...    -122.25            341300.0
4         3.8462       52.0  ...    -122.25            342200.0
5         4.0368       52.0  ...    -122.25            269700.0
6         3.6591       52.0  ...    -122.25            299200.0
7         3.1200       52.0  ...    -122.25            241400.0

[8 rows x 9 columns]







The prediction problem here is different in character from the electricity problem. There is no time axis. Each observation is independent — knowing the house value in Sacramento tells you nothing about the next row. What matters is the relationship between the eight features and the target. And that relationship is not linear. Census blocks near the coast behave differently from inland blocks even when income is held fixed. The geography matters in ways that do not reduce to a single variable.
Again: a formula you can write by hand will not capture this. You need a function that learns the pattern from the data.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 1  From Spreadsheet to Function
section: 2.3 What functions do that equations do not
text:
2.3 What functions do that equations do not
When you write a formula in a spreadsheet — say, =0.5 * B2 + 1200 — you are making a decision. You chose the slope. You chose the intercept. The formula does exactly what you told it to do, no more.
That is fine when the relationship is simple and known in advance. It fails when the relationship is complex, nonlinear, and discovered only by looking at the data.
This book is about the transition from formulas you write to functions that are fitted — determined by an optimization problem rather than by hand. The core idea is simple, even if the machinery is not: you describe what a good prediction looks like (a loss function), and you search for the function that makes predictions as good as possible (optimization).
Everything else — kernels, dual problems, support vectors — is in service of this idea.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 1  From Spreadsheet to Function
text:
2.4 Two problems, one framework
These two datasets are not chosen at random. They represent the two main structural types of prediction problem that appear in practice.
The electricity data is a time series: observations are ordered in time, and the recent past matters. Predicting tomorrow’s demand depends on what happened yesterday. Patterns repeat weekly and annually. The right model must respect time.
The California data is cross-sectional: observations are exchangeable, and there is no natural ordering. What matters is the geometry of the feature space — which inputs are similar, and whether similar inputs produce similar outputs.
The models developed in this book handle both. The optimization framework is the same. The loss function adapts to the measurement scale. The kernel decides what “similar” means.
By Part IV, you will have the tools to fit these datasets with SVR models that optimize percentage error directly — a choice that matters when the target varies over several orders of magnitude, as house prices and electricity demand both do.
For now, look at the data. Ask what it would mean to predict it well. The answer to that question will motivate every chapter that follows.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 1  From Spreadsheet to Function
text:
2.5 Exercises






NoteExercise 1.1 — Reading the time series





Load vic_elec and compute the average half-hourly demand separately for weekdays and weekends. Plot both on the same axis. At what time of day is the gap between weekday and weekend demand largest?
What you need: dplyr, lubridate, ggplot2.









NoteExercise 1.2 — The price of a bad prediction





Suppose electricity costs $150 per MWh during peak hours (5–9 PM weekdays) and $40 per MWh otherwise. Using the vic_elec data:

Compute the total cost if you always procure exactly what was demanded.
Compute the cost if your forecast is systematically 10% high during peak hours (you over-procure and the excess is wasted at zero value).
Compute the cost if your forecast is 10% low during peak hours (you under-procure and must buy the deficit at a spot premium of $500/MWh).

Which direction of error is more expensive? Does this change how you would define “a good prediction”?









NoteExercise 1.3 — Scale and error metrics





For the California Housing data, compute three prediction errors for a naive model that predicts the training mean for every observation:

MAE — mean absolute error (in dollars)
RMSE — root mean square error (in dollars)
MAPE — mean absolute percentage error (unitless)

Now repeat for a model that always predicts $200,000.
Which error metric is most informative? Does a $50,000 error mean the same thing for a $100,000 house as for a $600,000 house?
This question motivates Chapter 16.









NoteExercise 1.4 — Graduate level: loss functions as design choices





The choice of loss function is a statement about what errors matter.
Consider a delivery company predicting package transit times. A 2-hour error on a 4-hour delivery is qualitatively different from a 2-hour error on a 48-hour delivery — yet mean squared error treats them identically.
Formalize this argument. Define a loss function \(\ell(\hat{y}, y)\) that satisfies: (a) it is zero when prediction is perfect, (b) it is scale-free (invariant to multiplying both \(\hat{y}\) and \(y\) by a positive constant), and (c) it penalizes over-prediction and under-prediction symmetrically.
Is there more than one such function? What structural property do they share?
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
